title: The Macro Minute: Will China's Low-Cost AI Trigger A Hyperscaler Capex Reset?

Happy Monday out there, team. 42 macro
skipper here, Darius Dale here to
present our macro minute for Monday,
July 20th, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
will intensifying competition from
China's low-cost AI competitors cause US
hyperscalers to reassess their capital
allocation decisions?
The short answer is the risk of this
bearish outcome is as high as it's been
to date. No idea on timing. In terms of
analytical nuance, Alphabet and Tesla
open the reporting season for
Magnificent Seven constituents on
Wednesday. Microsoft, Meta, Apple, and
Amazon follow next week. Uh these names
comprise a quarter of the S&P 500 index.
Looking at Q2 earning season to date,
among 49 uh S&P 500 reporters, 92%
topped earnings estimates and 0% missed,
uh which is we have record in data going
back 2013. 74% companies uh positively
surprised on sales, while 6% missed, in
line with the previous quarter. Over 80
index uh members report this week.
Cost is a key driver of why China is
winning the US's manufactured, quote, AI
race at the inference layer. Energy is a
key strategic advantage for China as
well. China builds electricity capacity
faster than any country in human
history. For example, it added to in
2025, it added 543 gigawatts, exceeding
every nation's total electricity
generation capacity except the US, which
is mind-boggling.
A broad reassessment of hyperscale AI
CapEx represents a material risk to the
stock market in the context of
cyclically and structurally elevated
estimates for S&P 500 sales and earnings
growth. The good news is that S&P 500
sales and earnings growth are currently
on historical runs. There's a large
cohort of slower-moving investors that
will not sell on the first slowdown from
a rate of change perspective.
Despite efficiency gains at the training
layer, accelerating inference demands
means all these models are
memory-intensive, hence investors'
preferences for the quote, "picks and
shovels" that semiconductors represent.
Recall that the Philadelphia conductor
Philadelphia semiconductor index is
socks index jumped 105%
from its March trough to last month's
high. It has since dropped 20% entering
bare market territory. There's a dip to
be bought here, but perhaps not until
after the hyperscalers signal a
willingness to slow the growth of AI
CapEx into signs of robust competition
from China's low cost competitors.

our community. This was titled

skipper.

was the best argument I've heard raised
to continue to rely on risk management
system through this AI CapEx buildout.

race and the US energy industry is so
far behind in generating increased
capacity. They will
they will be able to outcompete us on

about So appreciate the work as always

second comment here.

guys.
You know this key takeaway is that you
know there's a lot of moving parts here
and this is just one theme that
investors need to be concerned about
right now. There's a you know there's
several orthogonal themes that are kind
of impacting financial markets not the
least of which is the you know kind of
the Fed regime change what that's going
to look like what the shapes and
contours of that what's the timing of
you know cyclical monetary policy
adjustments on the tightening side.
What's the timing of that regime change
or what does it look like from a dovish
or hawkish standpoint? Uh those are two
different uh debates. Obviously, the
inflation's going to be a key driver of
what that looks like both cyclically and
structurally. You have what's going on
in the Strait of Hormuz is a big deal uh
and so on and so forth. I mean, there's
just a lot going on for investors to try
to, you know, piece together that
puzzle. So, you know, with us it always
comes back down to, you know, where are
we in the six key macro cycles? You
know, are we headwinds or tailwinds in
those six key macro cycles? And more
importantly, uh from the perspective of
being in a in a in a risk-on market
regime, uh you know, your thought
process as a as an investor should be,
"Okay, what's the probability of having
a severe correction or crash whenever
you're in a risk-on market regime?" As a
risk manager in a risk-off market
regime, you need to be thinking about,
"What's the probability of having a
significant, you know, bounce or
sustained recovery uh in asset markets?"
And start to think about, "What could
change across those six key macro cycles
that could ultimately cause uh those
outcomes?" So, right now, as we've
talked about uh in the context of our
macro weather model, uh you know, we're
in a meaningful headwind. The macro
weather model is currently uh signaling
a meaningful headwind from the
perspective of the positioning cycle,
which ultimately means that if anything
goes wrong in the other five key macro
cycles being growth, inflation, monetary
policy, fiscal policy, and liquidity, if
anything goes wrong in those cycles, uh
then you're uh you're incrementally
wrong uh in the case of the growth
cycle, uh you're you're going to have
some issues. And so, this is why Q2
earning season, as we've been warning
about uh over the past few weeks, is a
risk of that it becomes a sell-the-news
catalyst for stocks. Uh and obviously,
we've seen a lot of the AI exposed
stocks, you know, already in drawdown
territory and that could deepen uh in
the coming weeks and months uh to the
extent we start to see some negative
uh uh revisions to AI CapEx. I'm not
We're not making that forecast. I mean,
I don't have any basis to make that
forecast in terms of it's going to
happen now. I don't think anybody really
has a basis for that, but that's neither
here nor there. The point we're making
is that the risk of this bearish outcome
is as high as it's been uh to date. So,
investors need to be aware of that. So,
we'll wrap it up there. Jerry Stahli
here presenting our macro minute from
Monday, July 20th, 2026. Best of luck
out there today. We'll catch you back
